title: We Are in This Dance Together: Gender, Power, and Globalization at a Mexican Garment Firm By Nancy Plankey-Videla. Rutgers University Press. 2012. 278 pages. $25.95 (paper)
abstract: Garment workers have long existed along the cutting edge of capital mobility and hyper-exploitation. From Manchester, England, to Lowell, Massachusetts; from New England to the American South; from the United States to Mexico; from Mexico to Honduras, China, and points beyond; garment manufacturers have long shifted factory locations from established production centers to the frontiers of capital production in an ongoing search for ever cheaper sources of laborers. At each stop, based upon gendered assumptions regarding both their suitability to the given work tasks and cheaper wage demands, women have been the preferred hires, thus starting anew the cycle of commodification, consciousness raising, radicalization, and capital flight. Nancy Plankey-Videla's recently published ethnographic study, We Are in This Dance Together: Gender, Power, and Globalization at a Mexican Garment Firm, brings her readers fresh insights into one of the more recent iterations of this temporal chain. Plankey-Videla's study centers around operations at the fictitiously named Moctezuma, a Mexican-owned high-end suit factory wherein she had open access to both management and workers, including a nine-month period of participant observation on the company's shop floor. Far from a fly-by-night operation, Plankey-Videla described Moctezuma as “a nationally and internationally recognized firm which operated in the same location for thirty-seven years” (4), and whose customer list during the late 1990s included international buyers such as Calvin Klein and J. C. Penney. Her goal as a feminist ethnographer was to “link macro processes and micro practices and to see how larger power dynamics affect the everyday lives of workers—especially those of women” (16). As with any case study, the value to its readers often centers on questions of the subject's historical uniqueness and/or generalizability. As such, the perceived value of This Dance may well hinge on Plankey-Videla's interpretation of her study's centripetal event, the Moctezuma strike of 2001, the first strike at the plant since 1972.
